Client 2: And I want it organized—accounts, beneficiaries, taxes. If we’re in that higher bracket, I don’t want avoidable mistakes.
Advisor: That makes sense. Let’s start high level and then we’ll go account by account. David, roughly what’s your household gross income per year right now—before taxes?
Client 1: I think it’s 110200 a year, gross.
Advisor: Got it—thank you. And on the spending side, about what do you two typically spend per month, all-in?
Client 2: We track it pretty closely—4000 a month.

Client 1: I think it’s 110200 a year, gross.
Advisor: Got it—thank you. And on the spending side, about what do you two typically spend per month, all-in?
Client 2: We track it pretty closely—4000 a month.
Advisor: Okay. Any regular debt payments—mortgage, car loans, credit cards you’re carrying month to month?
Client 1: No, nothing like that. Monthly debt payments are 0.

Client 2: And I want it organized—accounts, beneficiaries, taxes. If we’re in that higher bracket, I don’t want avoidable mistakes.
Advisor: That makes sense. Let’s start high level and then we’ll go account by account. David, roughly what’s your household gross income per year right now—before taxes?
Client 1: I think it’s 110200 a year, gross.
Advisor: Got it—thank you. And on the spending side, about what do you two typically spend per month, all-in?
Client 2: We track it pretty closely—4000 a month.

Client 1: It’s around a million, give or take.
Advisor: Okay—ballpark makes sense. Margaret, do you have the more exact total handy?
Client 2: Yes. Investable assets total is 965700.
Advisor: Great, thank you. Let’s break that into the actual buckets so I understand what’s where. First, do you have a joint taxable investment account—like a brokerage account you both own together?
Client 2: Yes, joint taxable. It’s on an advisor platform, and the value is 480050.

Client 2: There is. It’s in my name, held at a brokerage, and it’s 22500. And I’m with you—I don’t love anything opaque.
Advisor: Totally fair. We’ll make sure we understand what it is, what it costs, and whether it still earns its keep. Now, separate from investable assets, let’s talk about the home. David, you mentioned you own your primary residence—what do you estimate the value at?
Client 1: The house is 1.11M. It’s at the bank—well, the mortgage used to be, but it’s paid off now.
Advisor: Understood—so home value 1.11M, and no loan against it. On income sources—what’s actually coming in: do you have Social Security, pensions, dividends, interest?
Client 1: Social Security, yes, and then interest and dividends from the accounts.

Client 2: And I want it organized—accounts, beneficiaries, taxes. If we’re in that higher bracket, I don’t want avoidable mistakes.
Advisor: That makes sense. Let’s start high level and then we’ll go account by account. David, roughly what’s your household gross income per year right now—before taxes?
Client 1: I think it’s 110200 a year, gross.
Advisor: Got it—thank you. And on the spending side, about what do you two typically spend per month, all-in?
Client 2: We track it pretty closely—4000 a month.

Advisor: That makes sense. Let’s start high level and then we’ll go account by account. David, roughly what’s your household gross income per year right now—before taxes?
Client 1: I think it’s 110200 a year, gross.
Advisor: Got it—thank you. And on the spending side, about what do you two typically spend per month, all-in?
Client 2: We track it pretty closely—4000 a month.

Advisor: That makes sense. When markets drop, do you feel like you can stay the course, or does it start to keep you up at night?
Client 2: We can stay the course if we understand what’s happening, but yes—overall it’s moderate.
Advisor: Perfect. And just so you know how we work—my firm is an RIA, a registered investment advisor. Is that the advisory channel you’re expecting to work with, or were you looking for something more like a brokerage setup?
Client 1: No, that’s fine—RIA (registered investment advisor) is what we want.
Advisor: Great. One sec—just writing that down. And Margaret, anything you want to add on the risk side, like guardrails you’d want us to stick to?

Client 2: And I want it organized—accounts, beneficiaries, taxes. If we’re in that higher bracket, I don’t want avoidable mistakes.
Advisor: That makes sense. Let’s start high level and then we’ll go account by account. David, roughly what’s your household gross income per year right now—before taxes?
Client 1: I think it’s 110200 a year, gross.
Advisor: Got it—thank you. And on the spending side, about what do you two typically spend per month, all-in?
Client 2: We track it pretty closely—4000 a month.

Advisor: Perfect. So we’ve got income sources captured, the investment accounts and the house value, no liabilities, and the life insurance. Next time we can dig into how you want the portfolio to support spending and legacy without taking on swings that feel uncomfortable.
Client 1: That sounds good. I like having it clean—especially the “no loans” part.
Advisor: Totally. Before we wrap today, I want to capture a couple household basics so I don’t make assumptions. David and Margaret—are you two married?
Client 1: Yes, we’re married.
Advisor: Got it. And any dependents you’re still financially responsible for—kids, grandkids, anyone like that?

Advisor: Got it. And any dependents you’re still financially responsible for—kids, grandkids, anyone like that?
Client 2: No—number of dependents is 0.
Advisor: Okay, thank you. Switching gears a bit—when you think about investing, how would you describe your comfort with ups and downs? Conservative, moderate, aggressive—what fits best?
Client 1: I’d say moderate. I don’t want anything too wild.
Advisor: That makes sense. When markets drop, do you feel like you can stay the course, or does it start to keep you up at night?

Client 1: Thanks. I’d say… clarity. We’ve got plenty saved, but the income feels tighter than it should, and I don’t want Margaret stuck sorting it all out later.
Client 2: And I want it organized—accounts, beneficiaries, taxes. If we’re in that higher bracket, I don’t want avoidable mistakes.
Advisor: That makes sense. Let’s start high level and then we’ll go account by account. David, roughly what’s your household gross income per year right now—before taxes?
Client 1: I think it’s 110200 a year, gross.
Advisor: Got it—thank you. And on the spending side, about what do you two typically spend per month, all-in?

Advisor: Okay. And then retirement accounts—Margaret, I believe you have a retirement account at a bank, like a 401(k) or IRA—does that sound right?
Client 2: Yes, that one’s mine. It’s at the bank, and it’s 403300.
Advisor: Thanks. Any other retirement-type account that’s joint between you—maybe held on a retirement platform?
Client 1: Yes, we’ve got a joint one—more like a retirement platform account. The value is 59850.
Advisor: Got it. And I’m also seeing something that sounds like a private investment—private markets—Margaret, is there a smaller holding like that at a brokerage?

Advisor: Okay—so not one of the standard state options on my end, but we’ll keep it as Other. Thanks, Margaret.
Advisor: Next one is taxes. You mentioned being in a higher bracket—what bracket do you typically plan around right now?
Client 1: We plan around 35%.
Advisor: Got it. And just to make sure I’m not mixing federal and state in my head—when you say 35%, that’s the bracket band you want me to use for planning, right?
Client 2: Yes, 35%.

Advisor: Hi David, hi Margaret—good to meet you both. I’m glad you came in. Before we get into the weeds, what would make today’s meeting feel like a win for you?
Client 1: Thanks. I’d say… clarity. We’ve got plenty saved, but the income feels tighter than it should, and I don’t want Margaret stuck sorting it all out later.
Client 2: And I want it organized—accounts, beneficiaries, taxes. If we’re in that higher bracket, I don’t want avoidable mistakes.
Advisor: That makes sense. Let’s start high level and then we’ll go account by account. David, roughly what’s your household gross income per year right now—before taxes?
Client 1: I think it’s 110200 a year, gross.

Advisor: Perfect. Now, David, I know we already talked about the income sources—Social Security and interest and dividends—but I also need your gross annual income figure as a single number. What should I put down for you?
Client 1: I think it’s around sixty-two thousand a year—
Client 2: It’s more exact than that. David’s gross annual income is 62150.
Client 1: Yes, sorry—62150 is the number.
Advisor: Great, thank you—hang on, I’m just noting that. And just to keep us consistent, David: retired, occupation group retired, and you’re the primary in the household.

Client 1: Good—appreciate it.
Client 2: Yes, that’s what we want.
Advisor: David, Margaret—before we wrap, I want to make sure I’ve got your basic profile right. Can I confirm your work status these days—are you both retired?
Client 1: Yes, I’m retired.
Client 2: And I’m retired as well.

Client 1: Good—appreciate it.
Client 2: Yes, that’s what we want.

Client 2: It’s not a set paycheck, but annual interest and dividends is 37600.
